places.name: Tian Tian Hainanese Chicken Rice
places.address: Maxwell Food Centre, #01-10/11, 1 Kadayanallur Street, Singapore 069184
places.reviewSummary: The most famous chicken rice stall in Singapore, inside the buzzing Maxwell Food Centre. Silky poached chicken over fragrant rice cooked in chicken fat and pandan, with a fiery chilli-ginger sauce.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers swear by the tender chicken and aromatic rice; many warn the queue can stretch long and the stall sells out.
places.localTip: Go before noon or after 2pm to dodge the worst queue, and don't skip the chilli sauce — locals say it makes the dish.
places.story: Tian Tian has been poaching chicken at Maxwell since 1986, and a single endorsement from Anthony Bourdain turned a humble hawker stall into a global pilgrimage site. The secret is in the rice, simmered with chicken stock, garlic, ginger and pandan until every grain glistens. Gordon Ramsay once challenged the owner to a cook-off here and graciously admitted defeat.
places.highlight: Don't miss the chilli-ginger sauce on the side.

places.name: Jumbo Seafood (East Coast)
places.address: Blk 1206 East Coast Parkway, #01-07/08, East Coast Seafood Centre, Singapore 449883
places.reviewSummary: The seafront original of Singapore's most beloved chilli crab chain. Mud crabs smothered in a sweet-savoury tomato-chilli gravy you mop up with fried mantou buns.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners adore the chilli and black pepper crab and the breezy seaside setting; some flag steep prices and the need to book ahead.
places.localTip: Order the deep-fried mantou buns to soak up the gravy, and reserve a table by the water at sunset.
places.story: Jumbo opened on the East Coast in 1987, and its chilli crab helped cement a dish that the locals consider a national treasure. The gravy is a closely guarded balance of tomato, chilli and egg ribbons, and the crabs come straight from the tanks out front. On a warm night with the sea breeze and a cold Tiger beer, this is Singapore at its most indulgent.
places.highlight: Don't miss the black pepper crab if chilli isn't your thing.

places.name: Hawker Chan (Liao Fan Hong Kong Soya Sauce Chicken)
places.reviewSummary: The stall that became the world's cheapest Michelin-starred meal, famed for glossy soya sauce chicken over rice or noodles. A Chinatown rite of passage.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the lacquered chicken and bargain price, though some say the buzz outpaces the current consistency.
places.localTip: The original stall on Smith Street has shorter waits than the shophouse branch; the char siew is underrated.
places.story: Chan Hon Meng was a self-taught hawker when his soya sauce chicken rice earned a Michelin star in 2016, making global headlines as the cheapest starred dish on earth. The chicken is poached and basted in a soya master stock he refined over decades. Overnight, lines snaked down Chinatown's Smith Street, and a one-man stall became a worldwide brand.

places.name: Lau Pa Sat & Satay Street
places.reviewSummary: A Victorian cast-iron market hall ringed by hawker stalls; come dusk, Boon Tat Street closes to traffic and becomes a smoky open-air satay grill.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the atmospheric architecture and after-dark satay, though some find it touristy and a touch pricey.
places.localTip: Satay Street only fires up after 7pm; grab the chicken, mutton and prawn skewers and a tower of beer.
places.story: Lau Pa Sat means 'old market' in Hokkien, and its octagonal iron frame was shipped from Glasgow in 1894, a marvel of colonial engineering. By day it's a food court for the financial district's office crowd; by night the satay grillers spill onto the street in clouds of charcoal smoke. It's one of the few national monuments in the world where you can eat dinner under the original Victorian trusses.
places.highlight: Don't miss the satay after 7pm on Boon Tat Street.

places.name: Founder Bak Kut Teh
places.reviewSummary: A peppery Teochew-style pork rib soup beloved by locals and visiting celebrities alike. Tender ribs in a clear, garlicky, white-pepper broth with free refills.
places.googleReviewSummary: Fans rave about the peppery broth and fall-off-the-bone ribs; some note brusque service and a wait at peak hours.
places.localTip: Ask for a broth top-up — it's complimentary — and pair the ribs with you tiao (dough fritters) dunked in the soup.
places.story: Bak kut teh, literally 'meat bone tea,' was once a labourer's breakfast at the harbour, a cheap way to fortify a hard morning's work. Founder's Teochew version leans peppery rather than herbal, and a wall of celebrity photos attests to its fame. Locals still come at dawn for a steaming bowl, just as the dockworkers did a century ago.

places.name: Hjh Maimunah Restaurant
places.reviewSummary: A Bib Gourmand nasi padang spread where you point at dozens of Malay-Indonesian dishes. Beef rendang, sambal goreng and ayam bakar piled over rice.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the rich rendang and generous portions; some mention queues and that popular dishes run out early.
places.localTip: Come for lunch when the trays are freshest, and don't leave without the tahu telur (deep-fried egg tofu).
places.story: Named after its matriarch founder, Hjh Maimunah has been ladling out home-style Malay cooking in Kampong Glam since 1992. The kitchen still follows family recipes from the founder's village, and the rendang is slow-cooked for hours until the spices turn dark and deep. It's the kind of place where the queue is half locals, half tourists clutching guidebooks.
places.highlight: Don't miss the beef rendang and tahu telur.

places.name: Muthu's Curry
places.reviewSummary: A Little India institution famous for its tangy, tamarind-rich fish head curry. Big spreads of South Indian curries, biryani and crisp dosa.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners love the bold, spicy fish head curry and friendly service; a few find it pricier than neighbouring spots.
places.localTip: The fish head curry feeds two to three — the cheek meat is the prized bite, so dig in with your hands.
places.story: Muthu's started as a humble eatery in 1969 serving curries to Little India's labourers and has grown into one of Singapore's most decorated South Indian restaurants. Fish head curry itself is a Singaporean invention, born when Indian cooks adapted to local Chinese tastes for the fish head. The result is a only-in-Singapore dish that the city now claims as its own.

places.name: Hill Street Fried Kway Teow
places.address: Bedok South Market & Food Centre, 16 Bedok South Road, #01-187, Singapore 460016
places.reviewSummary: A legendary hawker stall serving smoky, wok-charred char kway teow with cockles, lap cheong and a perfect balance of sweet and savoury.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the wok hei and old-school flavour; many note the long queue and limited daily quantity.
places.localTip: Add the cockles if you eat them — they're the soul of an old-school plate; come early before it sells out.
places.story: Char kway teow was once a heart-attack-on-a-plate cooked by hawkers for hungry coolies, fried in pork lard over a roaring flame. This Bedok stall keeps the old-school recipe alive, its uncle hand-frying each plate to order for that elusive smoky wok hei. Regulars say the wait is part of the ritual.
places.highlight: Don't miss it with cockles for the authentic version.

places.name: Tiong Bahru Bakery
places.reviewSummary: The flagship of Singapore's hippest bakery, tucked among Tiong Bahru's art deco walk-ups. Buttery croissants, kouign-amann and serious coffee.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the flaky pastries and airy space; some say it gets crowded and seating is tight on weekends.
places.localTip: The kouign-amann sells out by midday — get there early, then wander the heritage Tiong Bahru estate.
places.story: When Tiong Bahru Bakery opened in 2012 with French baker Gontran Cherrier, it helped turn a sleepy 1930s housing estate into Singapore's most photographed neighbourhood. Tiong Bahru itself is the city's oldest public housing, all curved streamline-moderne facades and spiral staircases. Today the cafe and the old air-raid shelter nearby sit side by side, modern and historic in one block.
places.highlight: Don't miss the kouign-amann.

places.name: Chye Seng Huat Hardware
places.reviewSummary: A former hardware shop turned third-wave coffee complex with an in-house roastery, brew bar and leafy courtyard. The birthplace of Singapore's specialty scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the expertly brewed coffee and industrial-chic space; some find prices high and weekends busy.
places.localTip: Sit at the circular brew bar and ask the barista to recommend a single-origin pour-over.
places.story: Housed in a 1940s hardware store whose old signage was lovingly kept, 'CSHH' became the headquarters of PPP (Papa Palheta), the roasters credited with kickstarting Singapore's specialty coffee movement. The Jalan Besar shophouse row around it is a conservation gem of pre-war architecture. Order a cup and you're drinking from the lineage that taught the city to take coffee seriously.

places.name: Common Man Coffee Roasters
places.reviewSummary: A buzzy Robertson Quay roaster-cafe with a cult brunch menu and a barista academy upstairs. Big breakfasts, smashed avo and excellent espresso.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners love the brunch and consistent coffee; some mention wait times and a premium price for the area.
places.localTip: Weekday mornings are calmer than the weekend brunch crush; the smashed avocado and big breakfast are the staples.
places.story: Common Man opened in 2013 and quickly became the anchor of Robertson Quay's cafe scene, roasting its own beans and training a generation of Singaporean baristas. The riverside stretch outside, once a working godown wharf for spice traders, is now a leafy promenade of brunch spots. It's where expats and locals alike start a lazy weekend.

places.name: Ya Kun Kaya Toast (Far East Square)
places.address: 18 China Street, #01-01 Far East Square, Singapore 049560
places.reviewSummary: The flagship of Singapore's most famous kaya toast chain. Crisp charcoal-toasted bread spread with coconut-egg jam, soft-boiled eggs and kopi.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the nostalgic breakfast set and quick service; some say the flagship gets packed at peak hours.
places.localTip: Order the traditional set: kaya toast, two soft-boiled eggs with dark soy and pepper, and a kopi-o.
places.story: Ya Kun began in the 1940s as a humble coffee stall run by a Hainanese immigrant who learned to brew kopi the local way. The breakfast he perfected — charcoal-toasted bread, homemade kaya, runny eggs and sock-brewed coffee — is now a national ritual. This Far East Square flagship sits in restored shophouses where the city's first immigrant clan associations once gathered.
places.highlight: Don't miss dipping the toast into the soft-boiled eggs.

places.name: PS.Cafe Harding Road
places.reviewSummary: A glass-walled cafe in the jungly former barracks of Dempsey Hill, surrounded by rain trees. Famous truffle fries, brunch and a lush, breezy setting.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the verdant ambience and truffle fries; many note it's pricey and reservations are wise on weekends.
places.localTip: Sit on the outdoor deck under the rain trees, and the truffle shoestring fries are non-negotiable.
places.story: Dempsey Hill was a British army barracks and later the central manpower base before designers and restaurateurs moved into its black-and-white colonial buildings. PS.Cafe set up in a glass pavilion among the trees, and the surrounding greenery makes you forget you're minutes from Orchard Road. It's where Singaporeans escape the city without leaving it.
places.highlight: Don't miss the truffle shoestring fries.

places.name: Baba Chews Bar & Eatery
places.reviewSummary: A stylish cafe-eatery in the restored Joo Chiat Police Station, blending Peranakan flavours with all-day dining in a heritage Katong setting.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers enjoy the heritage building and Nyonya-leaning dishes; some say service can be slow when full.
places.localTip: Pair a visit here with a stroll down Koon Seng Road to see the candy-coloured Peranakan shophouses.
places.story: Baba Chews sits inside the old Joo Chiat Police Station, a 1928 building in the heart of Katong, the spiritual home of Singapore's Peranakan (Straits Chinese) community. The neighbourhood's pastel shophouses with their ornate tiles and shutters are some of the most photographed in the city. Here you can sip kopi where colonial-era policemen once kept watch.

places.name: Gardens by the Bay
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's futuristic green icon: towering Supertrees, the Cloud Forest and Flower Dome conservatories, and a nightly light show. A must-see.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are wowed by the Supertrees and domes; some warn of large crowds and that the conservatories require timed tickets.
places.localTip: The free Garden Rhapsody light show among the Supertrees runs nightly at 7:45pm and 8:45pm — arrive early for a spot.
places.story: Reclaimed from the sea and opened in 2012, Gardens by the Bay turned a barren bayfront into one of the world's most striking parks. The Supertrees are vertical gardens up to 50 metres tall that harvest solar energy and collect rainwater, a vision of the city as a 'City in a Garden.' The Cloud Forest dome holds a 35-metre indoor waterfall, the tallest of its kind.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Garden Rhapsody light show after dark.

places.name: Marina Bay Sands SkyPark Observation Deck
places.reviewSummary: The boat-shaped roof atop the three Marina Bay Sands towers, with a 360-degree observation deck 200 metres above the bay. Singapore's defining silhouette.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the panoramic skyline views; some say it's costly and the famous infinity pool is hotel-guests only.
places.localTip: The infinity pool is for hotel guests only — for the same view without the room, book the SkyPark deck or a CE LA VI drink.
places.story: Designed by architect Moshe Safdie and opened in 2010, Marina Bay Sands looks like a surfboard balanced on three skyscrapers. The SkyPark cantilevers further than the Eiffel Tower is wide, and its rooftop infinity pool is among the most photographed in the world. The whole complex rose on land reclaimed from the sea, a symbol of Singapore's relentless reinvention.

places.name: Merlion Park
places.reviewSummary: Home to the Merlion, the half-lion, half-fish statue spouting water into the bay. The classic Singapore photo spot facing Marina Bay Sands.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it a must-do photo stop with great bay views; many note it's small and very crowded.
places.localTip: Come at dawn for crowd-free photos, or after dark when the skyline and Marina Bay Sands light up behind the statue.
places.story: The Merlion was conceived in 1964 as a tourism emblem, fusing the lion of Singapura ('Lion City' in Sanskrit) with a fish tail nodding to its origins as a humble fishing village. The 8.6-metre statue was moved to its current waterfront perch in 2002 so it could spout directly into the bay. It has become the city's unofficial mascot, photographed millions of times a year.
places.highlight: Don't miss the forced-perspective shot 'drinking' the spout.

places.name: Buddha Tooth Relic Temple
places.reviewSummary: An ornate Tang-dynasty-style temple in Chinatown said to house a tooth relic of the Buddha. Gilded halls, a rooftop garden and a serene tea house.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the gold interior and peaceful rooftop; some remind that modest dress is required.
places.localTip: Cover shoulders and knees, then take the lift to the tranquil rooftop garden and its giant prayer wheel.
places.story: Completed in 2007 in the grand style of the Tang dynasty, this temple was built to enshrine what is believed to be a tooth relic of the Buddha, kept in a stupa made from over 300 kilograms of gold. Despite its antique look, it's one of Chinatown's newest landmarks. The hushed rooftop garden, with its enormous prayer wheel, offers a moment of calm above the bustle.

places.name: Raffles Hotel & Long Bar
places.reviewSummary: The grand colonial hotel where the Singapore Sling was invented. Sip the original cocktail at the Long Bar and toss peanut shells on the floor by tradition.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the colonial grandeur and the iconic Sling ritual; some say the bar is touristy and drinks are pricey.
places.localTip: The Long Bar is the only place in Singapore where you're invited to drop peanut shells on the floor — embrace it.
places.story: Opened in 1887 and named after Singapore's founder Stamford Raffles, this is the city's most storied hotel, where writers like Somerset Maugham and Rudyard Kipling once lodged. Around 1915 a bartender named Ngiam Tong Boon mixed the first Singapore Sling here, disguising gin in pink so ladies could drink in public. There was even, legend has it, a tiger shot under the billiard table.
places.highlight: Don't miss a Singapore Sling at the original Long Bar.

places.name: Sultan Mosque (Masjid Sultan)
places.reviewSummary: The golden-domed centrepiece of Kampong Glam, Singapore's Malay-Muslim heart. A majestic mosque surrounded by hip Haji Lane boutiques and Arab Street textile shops.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors admire the grand domes and welcoming guides; reminders are common to dress modestly and respect prayer times.
places.localTip: Robes are provided for visitors; afterwards explore Haji Lane's murals and the textile shops of Arab Street next door.
places.story: The first Sultan Mosque was built in 1824 with a grant from the East India Company, and the current grand structure dates to 1932. Look closely at the base of the golden domes and you'll see they rest on a band of dark glass bottle-ends, donated by poorer worshippers so everyone could share in the building. Around it, Kampong Glam buzzes with the city's best street art and Middle Eastern cafes.
places.highlight: Don't miss the murals and cafes of nearby Haji Lane.

places.name: Singapore Botanic Gardens
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's only UNESCO World Heritage Site: 160-year-old tropical gardens with a swan lake, rainforest patch and the dazzling National Orchid Garden.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the lush grounds and orchid display; some note the heat and that the orchid garden is ticketed.
places.localTip: Enter early to beat the heat, and pay the small fee for the National Orchid Garden — it's the highlight.
places.story: Founded in 1859, the Botanic Gardens became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2015, the first tropical garden so honoured. It was here that rubber-tree cultivation was pioneered in the 1890s, seeding the rubber boom that transformed Southeast Asia's economy. The National Orchid Garden holds over 1,000 species, including hybrids named for visiting dignitaries.
places.highlight: Don't miss the National Orchid Garden's VIP orchid hybrids.

places.name: Sri Mariamman Temple
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's oldest Hindu temple, founded in 1827, with a riotously colourful gopuram tower of sculpted gods. A vivid surprise in the heart of Chinatown.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are struck by the ornate tower and serene interior; reminders to remove shoes and dress modestly are common.
places.localTip: Look up at the gopuram tower to count the tiers of brightly painted deities; remove your shoes before entering.
places.story: Built in 1827 by immigrants from southern India, Sri Mariamman is Singapore's oldest Hindu temple, dedicated to the goddess of disease and protection. Its towering gopuram entrance, added later, swarms with hundreds of hand-painted figures from Hindu mythology. Each October the temple hosts Theemithi, a fire-walking festival where devotees stride across glowing coals.

places.name: Tiong Bahru Estate
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's oldest public housing estate, a leafy grid of curved 1930s streamline-moderne walk-ups now full of indie bookshops, cafes and murals.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the laid-back, hipster-meets-heritage vibe; some say the best spots are scattered and need a bit of walking.
places.localTip: Hunt down the heritage murals by local artist Yip Yew Chong tucked on the side walls of the old blocks.
places.story: Built in the 1930s by the colonial Singapore Improvement Trust, Tiong Bahru is the city's oldest housing estate, its curved balconies and spiral stairs inspired by aerodynamic 1930s design. There's a hidden air-raid shelter beneath one block, a relic of the war years. Today the same streets host one of the city's most beloved independent bookstores and a string of quiet cafes.
places.highlight: Don't miss the bird-singing corner where uncles hang songbird cages.

places.name: Tekka Centre
places.reviewSummary: The pulsing heart of Little India: a bustling wet market, fabric stalls and a hawker hall serving some of the city's best biryani, prata and Indian Muslim fare.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the cheap, authentic Indian food and lively chaos; some find it crowded and a bit overwhelming.
places.localTip: Come hungry on a Sunday when Little India is at its liveliest; the dum biryani and roti prata are the must-orders.
places.story: Tekka means 'bamboo' in Hokkien, a nod to the groves that once grew here, and the centre has been the social and culinary core of Little India for generations. Upstairs, sari and textile traders do brisk business; downstairs, the air is thick with cumin and ghee. On Sundays, migrant workers gather to share a taste of home.

places.name: Koon Seng Road Peranakan Houses
places.reviewSummary: A double row of candy-coloured Peranakan shophouses with intricate tiles, carved swing doors and pastel facades. The most beautiful street in the Katong/Joo Chiat district.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it a photographer's dream; reminders are common that these are private homes, so be respectful.
places.localTip: These are lived-in homes — admire from the street, keep voices low, and visit in the soft morning light for photos.
places.story: These ornate terraces were built in the early 1900s for the Peranakans, descendants of Chinese immigrants who married local Malays and blended both cultures into a unique Straits identity. Each facade drips with hand-painted ceramic tiles, phoenixes and peonies, and the swinging pintu pagar half-doors that let breeze in while keeping the street out. Joo Chiat became Singapore's first Heritage Town in 2011.
places.highlight: Don't miss the carved 'pintu pagar' swing doors up close.

places.name: Pulau Ubin
places.address: Pulau Ubin (bumboat from Changi Point Ferry Terminal, 51 Lorong Bekukong)
places.reviewSummary: A rustic island frozen in the 1960s, reached by bumboat from Changi. Rent a bike to explore last kampong villages, mangroves and the Chek Jawa wetlands.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the nostalgic, nature-filled escape from the city; some note basic facilities and that it's weather-dependent.
places.localTip: Bumboats leave when 12 passengers gather, so go on a weekend morning for shorter waits; rent a bicycle at the jetty.
places.story: Pulau Ubin offers a window into the Singapore that almost vanished, with wooden kampong houses, free-roaming chickens and granite quarries that once supplied the city's roads (its name means 'granite island'). The Chek Jawa wetlands reveal corals, seagrass and crabs at low tide. Riding a creaky bicycle down its dirt tracks feels like time travel.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Chek Jawa boardwalk at low tide.

places.name: Geylang Serai Market
places.reviewSummary: The cultural heart of Singapore's Malay community: a kampong-style market for nasi padang, kueh, textiles and the city's biggest Ramadan bazaar.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the authentic Malay food and lively atmosphere; some say it's busiest and best during Ramadan.
places.localTip: Visit during Ramadan for the legendary night bazaar, or any day for fresh kueh and the basement food centre.
places.story: Geylang Serai grew up around lemongrass (serai) plantations and became the spiritual home of Singapore's Malay community. The current market, built in a traditional kampong house style, anchors a neighbourhood alive with halal food and festivals. During Ramadan, its bazaar swells into the largest in the country, glowing with food stalls and fairy lights.

places.name: Mount Faber Park
places.reviewSummary: One of Singapore's oldest parks, a forested hilltop with panoramic harbour views and the cable car station to Sentosa. A breezy local escape.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the views and walking trails; some note the climb up and that the cable car is a separate paid ride.
places.localTip: Hike up via the Southern Ridges walk for free, then descend by cable car for the harbour view from above.
places.story: Mount Faber was named in 1845 after an army engineer and is one of the highest points in central Singapore. From its summit the whole harbour spreads out, once crowded with the trading ships that made the city rich. Today it's the gateway to the Southern Ridges trail and the swaying cable car that glides over the port to Sentosa.

places.name: Night Safari
places.reviewSummary: The world's first nocturnal wildlife park, where a tram glides through moonlit habitats of leopards, tapirs and elephants. A magical after-dark experience.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the unique nighttime tram and animal encounters; some say it gets crowded and the tram can feel rushed.
places.localTip: Book the earliest 7:15pm slot, do the walking trails on foot first, then take the tram to avoid the longest queues.
places.story: Opened in 1994, the Night Safari pioneered a whole new kind of zoo, using subtle moonlight-style lighting so visitors can watch nocturnal animals behave naturally after dark. Over 900 animals roam habitats designed without obvious bars or glass. Gliding silently past a pride of lions in the warm tropical night is an experience found nowhere else.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Creatures of the Night animal show.

places.name: Universal Studios Singapore
places.address: 8 Sentosa Gateway, Resorts World Sentosa, Singapore 098269
places.reviewSummary: Southeast Asia's only Universal Studios, with seven themed zones from Ancient Egypt to Far Far Away. Big rides, shows and movie-set photo ops on Sentosa.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the rides and themed zones; common gripes are long ride queues and high prices on peak days.
places.localTip: Arrive at opening and head straight to Battlestar Galactica or Transformers before the queues build; weekdays are quieter.
places.story: Opened in 2010 as part of Resorts World Sentosa, this is the only Universal Studios in Southeast Asia, packing seven movie-themed worlds onto a compact island site. Battlestar Galactica was once the world's tallest pair of duelling roller coasters. The whole resort sits on Sentosa, an island whose name ironically means 'peace and tranquillity' in Malay.

places.name: Southern Ridges & Henderson Waves
places.reviewSummary: A 10km chain of forested ridge-top trails linking parks via Henderson Waves, Singapore's highest pedestrian bridge with sculptural undulating timber waves.
places.googleReviewSummary: Hikers love the canopy views and the striking wave bridge; some note the heat and that it's a long, hilly walk.
places.localTip: Walk it late afternoon into golden hour, and Henderson Waves is gorgeous when its underbelly lights glow after dark.
places.story: Opened in 2008, the Southern Ridges stitched together a green corridor across the hills of southern Singapore, including the elevated Forest Walk that snakes through the tree canopy. Henderson Waves, 36 metres above the road, ripples in seven curved 'waves' of yellow balau wood with sheltered alcoves to rest in. It's an engineering sculpture you can walk across.
places.highlight: Don't miss the canopy-level Forest Walk section.

places.name: Singapore Flyer
places.reviewSummary: One of the world's largest observation wheels at 165 metres, with air-conditioned capsules giving 30-minute panoramic spins over Marina Bay and beyond.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders love the sweeping views, especially at sunset; some feel it's pricey for a single rotation.
places.localTip: Time your ride for sunset to catch both the daytime skyline and the city lights coming on in one rotation.
places.story: When it opened in 2008, the Singapore Flyer was the tallest observation wheel in the world, its 28 capsules each big enough to hold a small dinner party. On a clear day you can see all the way to the Malaysian coast and Indonesia's islands. It rises beside the Formula 1 Marina Bay street circuit, which roars to life under the wheel each September.

places.name: Jewel Changi Airport
places.reviewSummary: A glass dome at Changi Airport wrapped around the world's tallest indoor waterfall and a lush four-storey forest valley. A destination in its own right.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are stunned by the Rain Vortex and greenery; some say it's crowded and the upper attractions are ticketed.
places.localTip: The HSBC Rain Vortex runs a free light-and-sound show every evening — check the timings and watch from the basement level up.
places.story: Designed by Moshe Safdie and opened in 2019, Jewel transformed an airport carpark into a glass-and-steel biosphere. At its centre, the 40-metre HSBC Rain Vortex pours rainwater collected from the roof into a forest valley of 3,000 trees. It made flying through Changi a tourist attraction, and even non-travellers come just to stand beneath the falls.
places.highlight: Don't miss the nightly light show at the Rain Vortex.

places.name: CE LA VI SkyBar
places.address: 1 Bayfront Avenue, Level 57, Marina Bay Sands Tower 3, Singapore 018971
places.reviewSummary: A rooftop bar and club perched 57 floors up on Marina Bay Sands, offering the iconic skyline view without needing to be a hotel guest.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the unbeatable view and atmosphere; some note steep drink prices and a minimum spend.
places.localTip: There's a small SkyBar entry fee redeemable against drinks — far cheaper than the observation deck for the same vista.
places.story: CE LA VI sits atop the Marina Bay Sands SkyPark beside the famous infinity pool, and it's the most accessible way for non-guests to drink in that postcard view. As the sun sets, the financial district glitters and Gardens by the Bay glows below. By night it shifts from sky lounge to one of the highest dance floors in Singapore.
places.highlight: Don't miss arriving just before sunset for the full transition.

places.name: National Gallery Singapore
places.reviewSummary: The world's largest collection of Southeast Asian modern art, housed in the magnificently restored former Supreme Court and City Hall.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the stunning architecture and rich collection; some say it's vast and needs several hours to do justice.
places.localTip: Take the lift to the rooftop for free skyline views, then have a drink at Smoke & Mirrors on the terrace.
places.story: Opened in 2015 in Singapore's 50th year of independence, the National Gallery united two grand colonial landmarks, the old Supreme Court and City Hall, under a shimmering filigree roof. It was on these very steps that the Japanese surrendered in 1945 and Singapore later declared self-government. Inside hangs the definitive collection of Southeast Asian art, from colonial portraits to bold modernism.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rooftop terrace and skyline view.

places.name: ArtScience Museum
places.reviewSummary: A lotus-shaped museum at Marina Bay Sands fusing art, science and technology. Home to the dazzling permanent teamLab digital art wonderland.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the immersive teamLab show and striking building; some say tickets are pricey and weekends crowded.
places.localTip: Book the 'Future World' teamLab exhibit ahead — the interactive light rooms are spectacular and very photogenic.
places.story: Designed by Moshe Safdie to resemble a lotus flower or 'welcoming hand,' the ArtScience Museum opened in 2011 with ten finger-like galleries reaching skyward. Its roof channels rainwater down through the central atrium in a 35-metre indoor waterfall. The permanent teamLab Future World installation turns whole rooms into responsive seas of digital light.
places.highlight: Don't miss the teamLab Future World galleries.

places.name: Asian Civilisations Museum
places.reviewSummary: A riverside museum in the grand Empress Place Building tracing Asia's intertwined cultures, with a standout gallery on the Tang shipwreck treasure.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the well-curated galleries and the shipwreck cargo; some find it quieter and smaller than expected.
places.localTip: Head straight for the Tang Shipwreck gallery — 9th-century Chinese cargo recovered from the Java Sea.
places.story: Set in the 1860s Empress Place Building beside the Singapore River, this museum tells the story of the trade winds that made the region a crossroads. Its crown jewel is the Tang Shipwreck collection: tens of thousands of ceramics from a 9th-century Arab dhow that sank carrying Chinese goods west. It's a vivid reminder that Singapore has always been a port between worlds.

places.name: Peranakan Museum
places.reviewSummary: A jewel-box museum devoted to Peranakan culture, the unique Chinese-Malay heritage of the Straits, set in a restored old Chinese school.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the beautifully curated beadwork and ceramics; some say it's compact but exquisitely done.
places.localTip: Don't rush the wedding gallery — the intricate 12-day Peranakan wedding artefacts are the highlight.
places.story: Housed in the 1912 Old Tao Nan School, this museum reopened in 2023 after a full revamp celebrating the Peranakans, descendants of early Chinese settlers who married Malay locals. Their culture blends both worlds in dazzling beadwork, porcelain and cuisine. The galleries reveal everything from elaborate kebaya gowns to the marathon 12-day Straits wedding rituals.

places.name: Gillman Barracks
places.reviewSummary: A contemporary art enclave in leafy former British army barracks, packed with international galleries, the NTU CCA art centre and laid-back cafes.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the quiet, gallery-hopping vibe and greenery; some note that some galleries keep limited hours.
places.localTip: Go on a weekend afternoon when most galleries are open, and check for opening-night events with free drinks.
places.story: Built in 1936 and named after a British general, Gillman Barracks once housed colonial infantry before being reborn in 2012 as a contemporary art cluster. Its low colonial buildings, shaded by old rain trees, now hold cutting-edge galleries from around the world. It's an unhurried, green counterpoint to the city's gleaming museums.

places.name: Haji Lane Street Art
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's most colourful lane, a narrow Kampong Glam alley lined with indie boutiques, hip bars and vivid wall murals. Instagram heaven.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the murals and quirky shops; some say it's narrow and gets very crowded on weekend evenings.
places.localTip: Come on a weekday morning for mural photos without the crowd, then grab mezze at a Haji Lane rooftop after dark.
places.story: Haji Lane was once a quiet residential back lane in the Malay-Arab quarter, named for the pilgrims who once lodged here before sailing to Mecca. In the 2000s independent designers moved into its tiny shophouses, and street artists turned the walls into a riot of colour. Today it's the city's most photographed alley, equal parts boutique strip and open-air gallery.
places.highlight: Don't miss the large mural at the Haji Lane and Beach Road corner.

places.name: Thian Hock Keng Temple
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's oldest and most ornate Hokkien temple, built in 1840 without a single nail. Intricately carved to honour Mazu, goddess of the sea.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors admire the exquisite carvings and serene courtyards; some note it's smaller than expected but a hidden gem.
places.localTip: Walk behind the temple to see the 44-metre 'Singapore's Story' mural depicting early Hokkien immigrant life.
places.story: Completed in 1840 facing what was then the seafront, Thian Hock Keng was built by Hokkien immigrants to thank Mazu, the sea goddess, for their safe voyage from China. Master craftsmen assembled it using interlocking joints and not a single nail, with materials shipped from China. Behind it, a sweeping modern mural tells the story of the immigrants who once stepped ashore right here.
places.highlight: Don't miss the nail-free construction and dragon-tiled roof.

places.name: Esplanade - Theatres on the Bay
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's spiky 'durian' arts centre on the bay, hosting world-class concerts plus a generous programme of free live music daily.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the architecture and free performances; some say the concert hall acoustics are the real highlight.
places.localTip: Catch the free nightly gigs at the outdoor theatre and concourse — no ticket needed — then enjoy the rooftop bay view.
places.story: Opened in 2002, the Esplanade is wrapped in over 7,000 aluminium sunshades that locals instantly nicknamed 'the durian' for its spiky resemblance to the pungent fruit. Beneath that shell sits one of Asia's finest concert halls. True to its mission, it stages thousands of free performances a year, from jazz to Indian classical, on its outdoor stages.
places.highlight: Don't miss the free performances on the outdoor stage.

places.name: Timbre+ One-North
places.address: 73A Ayer Rajah Crescent, JTC LaunchPad, Singapore 139957
places.reviewSummary: A buzzy outdoor gastropark fusing hawker-style food trucks, craft beer and nightly live bands from Singapore's home-grown music scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the live music, food variety and casual vibe; some note it gets loud and packed after work.
places.localTip: Bands usually start around 8pm; come on a weeknight for a relaxed crowd and grab a craft beer from the airstream bar.
places.story: Timbre helped pioneer the live-music-and-good-food formula in Singapore, championing local bands at a time when the scene was small. The One-North outpost sits among tech startups and research labs in a converted industrial estate. It pairs modernised hawker fare with home-grown musicians, a hangout where engineers and artists share long communal tables.

places.name: Maduro
places.reviewSummary: An elegant, hushed listening bar tucked in a residential corner, dedicated to live jazz and serious cocktails in a clubby, old-world setting.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the superb live music and refined ambience; some note it's intimate so booking ahead is essential.
places.localTip: Reserve ahead and check the live-music nights; the room is small, so an early booking gets you close to the band.
places.story: Named after a cigar, Maduro is the passion project of design hotelier Loh Lik Peng, conceived as a grown-up listening room where the music comes first. Velvet, dark wood and low lights set the mood for live jazz and blues most nights. It's the kind of speakeasy locals keep half-secret, a world away from the bayfront clubs.

places.name: The Projector
places.address: 6001 Beach Road, #05-00 Golden Mile Tower, Singapore 199589
places.reviewSummary: A beloved independent cinema in a retro 1970s tower that doubles as a venue for indie gigs, album launches and quirky live events.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the nostalgic charm, indie films and live events; some say seating is vintage and a touch worn.
places.localTip: Check the events calendar for live shows in the Green Room, and grab a drink at the in-house bar before the gig.
places.story: The Projector revived a faded 1970s cinema in the brutalist Golden Mile Tower, keeping the original raked seats and screen. Beyond arthouse films, it became a hub for Singapore's indie music scene, hosting intimate gigs and listening parties. It's a labour of love that proves the city's underground culture is alive and kicking.

places.name: Crazy Elephant
places.address: 3E River Valley Road, #01-03/04 Clarke Quay, Singapore 179024
places.reviewSummary: A long-running riverside dive bar at Clarke Quay famed for nightly live blues and rock and a refreshingly unpretentious, graffiti-covered vibe.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the genuine live blues and laid-back feel; some note it's small, loud and gets smoky-busy at weekends.
places.localTip: The house band cranks up nightly — grab a riverside table outside if you want the music a little softer.
places.story: Crazy Elephant has been pumping out live blues and rock at Clarke Quay since 1993, outlasting countless trendier neighbours along the river. Its graffiti-scrawled walls and no-frills attitude make it a holdout of grit in a polished entertainment district. Clarke Quay itself was a 19th-century godown wharf where bumboats unloaded cargo onto the riverbank.

places.name: Zouk
places.address: 3C River Valley Road, The Cannery, Singapore 179022
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's most famous nightclub, a multi-room superclub at Clarke Quay drawing top international DJs and a serious dance crowd since 1991.
places.googleReviewSummary: Clubbers praise the world-class DJs and sound system; some mention long entry queues and steep drink prices.
places.localTip: Wednesday's Mambo Jambo night with retro hits is a Singapore institution — expect synchronised crowd dance moves.
places.story: Founded in 1991 in a row of converted riverside warehouses, Zouk grew from a single room into one of Asia's most respected clubbing brands, regularly ranked among the world's best. It launched the careers of local DJs and hosted nearly every big name in dance music. Its weekly retro 'Mambo' night, with its own crowd choreography, is a rite of passage for generations of Singaporeans.
places.highlight: Don't miss Mambo Jambo night on Wednesdays.

places.name: Atlas Bar
places.address: 600 North Bridge Road, Parkview Square, Singapore 188778
places.reviewSummary: A breathtaking art deco lobby bar with a three-storey golden gin tower holding over 1,300 gins. Routinely ranked among the world's best bars.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests are dazzled by the Gatsby-esque interior and martinis; some note a dress code and a wait without a booking.
places.localTip: There's a smart dress code (no shorts or flip-flops); come early evening for the champagne afternoon, or book ahead for cocktails.
places.story: Atlas occupies the soaring lobby of Parkview Square, a 1990s skyscraper built to evoke 1920s Gotham, complete with bronze statues and a 15-metre ceiling. Its centrepiece is a gilded tower stacked with one of the world's largest gin collections, reached by a rolling library ladder. Stepping in feels like walking onto the set of a Jazz Age film.
places.highlight: Don't miss the gin tower and a classic martini.

places.name: Smoke & Mirrors
places.address: 1 St Andrew's Road, #06-01 National Gallery Singapore, Singapore 178957
places.reviewSummary: A rooftop cocktail bar atop the National Gallery with a jaw-dropping head-on view of the Padang, Marina Bay and the city skyline.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the unbeatable skyline view and inventive drinks; some say it's pricey and best booked at sunset.
places.localTip: Reserve a terrace table for sunset to watch the Marina Bay skyline light up; the cocktail menu changes seasonally.
places.story: Perched on the roof of the National Gallery, set inside the former Supreme Court and City Hall, Smoke & Mirrors looks straight across the historic Padang field to Marina Bay Sands. The artful cocktails play tricks of presentation that live up to the name. It's one of the few places where you sip drinks above two of the nation's most important colonial buildings.
places.highlight: Don't miss the terrace view at golden hour.

places.name: 28 HongKong Street
places.reviewSummary: An unmarked speakeasy behind a plain shopfront, repeatedly ranked among Asia's and the world's 50 best bars. American-style drinks and a buzzy late vibe.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the expert cocktails and cool hidden vibe; some say it's tricky to find and reservations help.
places.localTip: There's no sign — look for the door at number 28 and ring; book ahead as walk-in space fills fast on weekends.
places.story: Opened in 2011 in a conservation shophouse near Boat Quay, 28 HongKong Street pioneered Singapore's speakeasy boom, hiding world-class cocktails behind an anonymous facade. It has appeared on Asia's 50 Best Bars list year after year. The street itself was named for the Hong Kong-born merchants who settled along the river in the colonial era.

places.name: Marquee Singapore
places.address: 2 Bayfront Avenue, #B1-67 The Shoppes at Marina Bay Sands, Singapore 018972
places.reviewSummary: Singapore's largest nightclub, a three-storey spectacle inside Marina Bay Sands complete with an indoor Ferris wheel and a spiral slide.
places.googleReviewSummary: Clubbers love the over-the-top production and big-name DJs; some mention high prices and strict door policy.
places.localTip: Dress to impress for the door, and the eight-seat Ferris wheel ride inside is the photo everyone comes for.
places.story: Marquee opened at Marina Bay Sands in 2019 as the city's biggest club, importing the New York nightlife brand and spending lavishly on spectacle. It packs a Ferris wheel and a three-storey slide onto its dance floors, with international headline DJs most weekends. It's clubbing as Vegas-style theatre, fittingly housed in Singapore's grandest casino resort.
places.highlight: Don't miss a spin on the indoor Ferris wheel.

places.name: Junior The Pocket Bar
places.reviewSummary: A pint-sized cocktail bar that completely reinvents its theme and menu every few months, from Cuban rum dens to tropical escapes. Big creativity, tiny room.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the inventive rotating concepts and friendly bartenders; many note it's truly tiny so seats go fast.
places.localTip: Seats are limited and it transforms its whole theme every few months — check what concept is running before you go.
places.story: Junior is exactly what its name suggests: a 'pocket bar' barely bigger than a living room in a Tanjong Pagar shophouse. Its gimmick is reinvention, tearing down and rebuilding its entire theme, decor and drinks list a few times a year. The result is a bar locals keep returning to just to see what it has become.

places.name: Boat Quay
places.reviewSummary: A curved row of restored shophouses along the Singapore River packed with bars and pubs, glowing with skyline views and after-work crowds.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the lively riverside setting and views; some warn of pushy touts and tourist-priced drinks.
places.localTip: Walk past the touts at the entrance to the quieter far end for better-value drinks and the best skyline view.
places.story: Boat Quay was once the beating heart of colonial Singapore's trade, where three-quarters of the port's shipping business was handled along its riverbank godowns. The curve of the river here was said to resemble the belly of a carp, an auspicious sign for prosperity. The old warehouses now hold the bars where the modern city comes to unwind.

places.name: 1-Altitude Coast
places.reviewSummary: A breezy seafront rooftop bar on the Sentosa boardwalk, prized for sunset cocktails over the harbour and a relaxed, party-leaning evening crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the sunset views and chilled vibe; some note drinks are premium-priced and it's weather-dependent.
places.localTip: Arrive for sunset over the harbour, then stay as the DJ sets ramp up and the city lights come on across the water.
places.story: The 1-Altitude name became famous for once running the world's highest open-air bar atop a downtown tower. Its Sentosa outpost trades sky-high heights for a seafront perch on the resort island's boardwalk. Here the view is of cargo ships gliding through one of the planet's busiest harbours as the sun drops into the strait.
places.highlight: Don't miss the harbour sunset before the DJ sets begin.

places.name: The Intan
places.reviewSummary: A private Peranakan home-museum where collector Alvin Yapp shares his treasures over tea and kueh in an intimate, by-appointment experience.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests adore the personal, passionate storytelling and beautiful antiques; many call it a one-of-a-kind hidden gem.
places.localTip: Book well ahead — it's strictly by appointment, and the experience includes Nyonya tea and home-style snacks.
places.story: The Intan is the lifelong passion of Alvin Yapp, who turned his Joo Chiat home into an award-winning private museum of Peranakan culture. Wander among beaded slippers, porcelain and ancestral furniture as he tells the stories behind each piece over tea. It's less a museum than an invitation into a living heritage household, hosted by the collector himself.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Nyonya tea and kueh that come with the tour.

places.name: Food Playground
places.reviewSummary: A hands-on cooking school in a Chinatown shophouse teaching iconic local dishes like laksa, chilli crab and chicken rice, with cultural context.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the fun, well-run classes and warm instructors; many say they leave able to recreate the dishes at home.
places.localTip: Tell them dietary needs in advance — they happily adapt recipes — and you eat everything you cook.
places.story: Food Playground was founded to share the multicultural roots of Singapore's cuisine, where Chinese, Malay and Indian traditions blend on one plate. In a restored Chinatown shophouse, instructors walk you through pounding spice pastes and balancing the sweet-sour-spicy notes that define local cooking. You leave understanding not just how a dish is made, but why it tastes the way it does.

places.name: Batik Painting Workshop (Arch & Hue)
places.reviewSummary: A hands-on batik art workshop in the Malay-Arab quarter where you learn the wax-resist dyeing technique and take home your own painted fabric.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants love the relaxing, creative session and patient guides; many highlight taking home a finished piece.
places.localTip: Wear clothes you don't mind staining, and pair the class with a wander down palm-lined Bussorah Street outside.
places.story: Batik, the ancient Southeast Asian art of patterning cloth with wax and dye, is woven into the Malay heritage of Kampong Glam. In a workshop on tree-lined Bussorah Street, in the shadow of the Sultan Mosque, you learn to draw with hot wax before flooding the fabric with colour. It's a meditative craft that connects you to a regional tradition centuries old.

places.name: Scent by SIX
places.address: Eng Hoon Street, Tiong Bahru, Singapore 169787
places.reviewSummary: A boutique perfumery in heritage Tiong Bahru where you blend your own bespoke fragrance under a perfumer's guidance and bottle it to take home.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the personalised, hands-on scent-making and elegant space; many call it a memorable, unique keepsake.
places.localTip: Sessions are intimate and best booked ahead; come with an idea of scents you love to speed up your blend.
places.story: Scent by SIX turns perfume-making into a hands-on craft, guiding you through top, heart and base notes until you've composed a fragrance that's wholly yours. The studio sits among the curved 1930s walk-ups of Tiong Bahru, the city's most characterful old neighbourhood. You walk out with a bottle of a scent that exists nowhere else in the world.

places.name: Rochor Centre site & Rainbow Flats (Dakota Crescent)
places.address: Old Airport Road, Dakota Crescent, Singapore 390095
places.reviewSummary: One of Singapore's oldest surviving housing estates, with a beloved retro dove playground and quiet 1950s blocks loved by photographers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the nostalgic mid-century architecture and vintage playground; some note it's residential and low-key.
places.localTip: The iconic dove-shaped sand playground is the money shot — go in soft morning light and be respectful of residents.
places.story: Dakota Crescent, built in 1958, is among the oldest public housing estates still standing in Singapore, named after the Dakota aircraft that once flew from the nearby old airport. Its centrepiece is a sculptural dove-shaped playground, a cherished relic of the era when playgrounds were works of art. Conservation efforts saved it as a window into the city's young nation-building years.
places.highlight: Don't miss the vintage dove playground.

places.name: Rochor Centre / People's Park & Pinnacle@Duxton Skybridge
places.reviewSummary: The 50th-floor skybridge atop the Pinnacle@Duxton, the world's tallest public housing, offering a 360-degree photographer's view over the city and port.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the sweeping rooftop panorama for a tiny fee; some note entry is capped daily and via tapping a transit card.
places.localTip: Entry is limited per day and paid by tapping an EZ-Link transit card at the gate — go on a clear late afternoon.
places.story: The Pinnacle@Duxton is the world's tallest public housing development, seven 50-storey towers linked by sky bridges, built on the site of Singapore's first high-rise flats. Its top-floor sky garden is open to visitors for a small fee, offering a rare rooftop panorama from inside an ordinary residential block. It's the perfect symbol of a city where even public housing reaches for the sky.
places.highlight: Don't miss the full loop of the 50th-floor sky garden.

places.name: Old Hill Street Police Station
places.reviewSummary: A grand colonial building famous for its facade of 927 rainbow-coloured window shutters. One of the most photographed buildings in Singapore.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the vivid rainbow windows and central location; some note the interior galleries are small and quiet.
places.localTip: Shoot the rainbow windows from across Hill Street or the river for the full spectrum; mornings have the best light.
places.story: Built in 1934 as a police station and barracks, this neo-classical landmark was once one of the largest government buildings in the colony, even featuring an early lift. Its 927 windows were later painted in the full rainbow, turning a stately facade into a riot of colour. Today it houses government arts offices and free galleries behind those famous shutters.
places.highlight: Don't miss framing all the rainbow shutters from across the river.

places.name: Helix Bridge
places.reviewSummary: A pedestrian bridge curling across Marina Bay in a double-helix of steel, lit up at night and framing perfect skyline and Marina Bay Sands photos.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the futuristic design and night views; some say it gets crowded around the light-show hours.
places.localTip: Stand in the viewing pods along the bridge after dark to frame Marina Bay Sands and the ArtScience Museum together.
places.story: Opened in 2010, the Helix Bridge was designed in the shape of a DNA double helix, the first such curved structure of its kind, built from over 600 tonnes of duplex stainless steel. At night, coloured letters spelling out the bases of DNA glow along its spirals. It's both an engineering showpiece and the best vantage point for capturing the bay's skyline.
places.highlight: Don't miss the bridge lit up after sunset.

places.name: Sri Veeramakaliamman Temple
places.reviewSummary: One of Singapore's most vivid Hindu temples, its gopuram tower swarming with brightly painted deities. The most photogenic spot in Little India.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are dazzled by the colourful sculptures and devotional energy; reminders to remove shoes and dress modestly are common.
places.localTip: Visit during evening prayers for incense, drums and atmosphere; photograph the gopuram from across Serangoon Road.
places.story: Dedicated to the fierce goddess Kali, this temple was built in 1881 by Indian labourers who worked the lime pits and cattle yards of early Little India. Its towering gopuram and inner shrines explode with colour, every figure hand-painted in vivid hues. It survived the bombs of the Second World War intact, which devotees took as a sign of the goddess's protection.
places.highlight: Don't miss the gopuram at dusk during evening prayers.

places.name: Cloud Forest Dome
places.address: 18 Marina Gardens Drive, Gardens by the Bay, Singapore 018953
places.reviewSummary: A cool, misty conservatory built around a 35-metre indoor mountain and waterfall, draped in orchids and ferns. A surreal, photogenic green world.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the towering waterfall and lush planting; some note timed tickets and crowds at peak times.
places.localTip: Take the lift to the top of the mountain and walk down the spiralling skywalks for the best photos looking out over the mist.
places.story: Inside Gardens by the Bay, the Cloud Forest recreates the cool, damp highlands of a tropical mountain, complete with one of the world's tallest indoor waterfalls cascading 35 metres down a green-clad peak. Walkways spiral around the 'Cloud Mountain' through perpetual mist. It's a feat of climate engineering that lets you photograph a misty cloud forest in the middle of equatorial Singapore.
places.highlight: Don't miss the misty skywalk near the top of the mountain.
